item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey Vsacue, Michael here. Now, where are your fingers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot of stuff this SCP has going for it — the anomaly being based around a recognizable and beloved Internet figure like Vsauce does a great job of piquing the readers' interest, and the transcripts of the videos, while a bit cliché (the first video's use of word salad and description of the "others" in particular felt like something I'd already read in countless other SCP articles) successfully managed to establish their own unique flavor and creep me out a decent amount (the third video implying but not outright describing "Michael" just staring at the camera for minutes on end worked wonders for me specifically.) However, I felt like the quality of the article began to dip substantially after the Michael cult was established; the interview with the cultist didn't really strike me as anything I hadn't seen before in other articles, and the final interview with Michael himself, while setting up some marginally interesting questions, didn't really go anywhere and felt rather lackluster as a way to close out the article. Personally, I feel like the article works a lot better without the introduction of the cult entirely; the simple, understated WTF factor of anomalous Vsauce videos appearing on the Internet, combined with the silent implication that some sort of alien entity (or entities) is mimicking Michael and spreading nonsensical information for some unknown reason, interests me far more than people deciding to become Michael Stevens because of an unexplained sense of idolatry towards him. Novote, because this skip is definitely charming and impressive (especially for a first article), but there are a handful of things holding it back.
Edit: After reading this skip I ended up embarking on yet another Vsauce binge, so the article definitely has that going for it as well lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every second you don't spend running Micheal just gets closer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hey vsauce, michael here
-from inside your head- what if you were defenseless?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heeyyyy, VSauce! SCP-4515-1 here…

And now we wait for Michael to be shown this page and decide to troll the SCP readership by recording as exact-as-possible recreations of the videos in those logs and uploading them to novel accounts on Youtube and other video sites (though probably not the porn or pirate sites, especially as TPB has been taken down)…

item.title: spit facts novote
item.content:encoded: i like the idea of using this as the basis of an anomaly, i like the twist at the end, but most of it felt like "recognizable character being chaotic and creepypasta" so ultimately i felt indifferent to the whole thing. VERY good for your first scip tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this gets off to a really strong start with the first video, but everything that comes up afterwards doesn't feel like it elaborates on things sufficiently, seeming like it gets distracted with layering on weird and spooky elements almost at-random.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dunno how you managed to make Vsauce spooky, but congratulations, you got my upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The insistence on using "the host of Vsauce" instead of "Michael Stevens" feels absurd. There's no reason the Foundation wouldn't use his name.
I don't dislike the article, though I'm probably missing something as I've never watched a Vsauce video.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this! My only nitpick is that the Foundation probably wouldnt call websites by their names like "YouTube" or "xHamster" but instead write out the url. Thats just my opinion. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did mention that when I said there had to be a twist to the original content but I was a little vague. I was aware that there were quite a few pop culture SCP on the main list, I just couldn't remember any specific ones. I do remember an SCP involving We Are Number One that was similar to this, but I couldn't remember any specifics. I could've just looked it up and found that it was SCP-3006,but I didn't think about that. I originally wrote about 3006, but I was way too vague and confusing, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addendum 4515.2: Incident Report 4515-Alpha:
On 11/12/201█, reports of cult activity located inside an abandoned warehouse emerged in Kansas City, Missouri. Foundation agents were sent to investigate while a disinformation campaign and general Class-A amnestics were deployed to the local population as needed.

There needs to be some explanation/justification of what caused Foundation agents to be sent to investigate before local law enforcement. Most 'cult activity' would probably boil down to teenagers screwing around, there would need to be evidence otherwise for the Skippers to send a team, especially already looking to amnesticize people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m on the fence about it, but overall I think it just about squeaked an upvote due to means by which the anomaly is conveyed. It’s cute in an arguably disturbing anomaly, I’d just wish that the occult aspect was less predictable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey man, I’m not staff, so don’t take my word for gospel but articles that are about pop culture media are allowed on the mainlist.

It’s only one-for-one SCP versions of pop culture things (say an article that’s just a lightsaber, or one that’s just the Delorean from BTtF) that belong as -Js (if they even get enough upvotes to stay as a -J, it’s harder than you’d guess to write joke stuff)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello, and Welcome! I'm not an expert in writing SCP pages (I don't even remember if I have any, since I have been inactive for a while), but I'll try to give my best feedback anyways.

SCP's written about pop culture typically belong in the Joke SCP category (any article with -J at the end if you didn't know). A lot of Joke SCP's still aren't accepted, because a lot of them are just generally (maybe a bit vaguely ) trash.
ItThat doesn't mean all pop culture SCP automatically go to being joke SCP's (the only one that comes to mind is SCP-1733 ), but are few and far between. SCP's written in this style have to add a twist to their source material. While the bass of your SCP involves a cult about VSauce, 1733 is about a recording of one basketball game. The main difference between the two is that 1733 isn't about the basketball game, meaning you could theoretically replace it with a VSauce video and change the references to the basketball game. Read 1733 if you want to understand.

Speaking of the cult aspect, a core part of SCP writing is understanding it much more than the reader can with just that page. If you don't know something you hint about, that isn't mystery, it's just lazy writing. You have to know what you hide (goes with blacked out text too). I have a feeling that you don't actually know what the cult did.

That's all the tips that I have. Just what I've gathered over time.

Wow, that was long

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My main gripe is that the escalation from topic to topic in the first few episodes didn't sound like Vsauce at all. It was a bit too rigid, and doesn't flow into different subjects smoothly like the original vsauce.

I totally can see this as intentional, as this isn't the real michael stevens, but it still bugged me slightly. Besides that, this is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello friends, I'm here with my first ever SCP article.

Thank you so much to those who helped me make this: shoutouts to my good friend CannedBread for helping me with my drafts, my other good friend Alex for helping me with creating some ideas for it, and RockTeethMothEyes in the draft and critique forum for the thorough feedback.

The thumbnail image is a combination of this DNA image by Joseluissc3 and this hieroglyph by William Crochot (and converted to an SVG file by Medium69), both under CC-BY-SA-4.0.
